_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning about how historical events, individuals, groups, or institutions from the medieval or early modern period have been interpreted over time, and what legacies they left.

_source.w: We are learning about how historical events, individuals, groups, or institutions from the medieval or early modern period have been interpreted over time, and what legacies they left.
y: Historical interpretations are shaped by the values and concerns of the time in which they are written.
Understanding how interpretations change helps us read history more critically and think for ourselves.
The legacies of this period — in governance, science, art, and philosophy — are all around us.
eg: Investigating how the Crusades have been interpreted differently by European and Islamic historians.
Examining how the legacy of the Renaissance is assessed — was it a European phenomenon or did it build on global knowledge?
Exploring how colonial legacies are being reinterpreted in light of Indigenous perspectives.
Discussing how the same historical figure is viewed as a hero in one tradition and a villain in another.
Analysing how popular culture interpretations of medieval life compare with historical evidence.
Comparing interpretations of a historical institution from different scholarly traditions.
